---
format:
  pdf:
    lang: en
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    keep-tex: false
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "Arial"
    fontenc: "T1"
    inputenc: "utf8"
    fontsize: 10pt
    title-block-style: none
    colorlinks: true
    urlcolor: blue
    linkcolor: blue
    geometry: "top=1.5cm, bottom=2cm, left=2cm, right=2cm"
    papersize: letter
    microtype: true
    linestretch: 1.0
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \newcommand{\cvsection}[1]{%
          \vspace{-1.5em}
          \section*{\fontsize{11}{13}\selectfont\underline{\makebox[\linewidth][l]{#1}}}%
          \vspace{-1.3em}
        }
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \singlespacing
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \setlist{itemsep=0pt, parsep=0pt, topsep=0pt, partopsep=0pt}
---

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
  {\large \textbf{Luis E. Mart\'{i}nez-Rodr\'{i}guez, Ph.D.}}\\[0.5ex]
  \normalsize
  Curriculum Vitae \\[0.5ex]
  \href{https://linkedin.com/in/luis-martinez-1394b619a}{linkedin.com/in/luis-martinez-1394b619a} \\
  luised94@mit.edu \,\textbar\, \href{https://github.com/luised94}{github.com/luised94}
\end{center}
\cvsection{Researcher Profile}
\begin{center}
Protein Purification | Genetic Engineering | NGS Analysis
\end{center}
\vspace{-1.3em}
Ph.D. with 9+ years of expertise across protein and nucleic-acid biochemistry, molecular biology, and yeast genetics. Specialization in DNA replication mechanisms. Highly proficient at biochemical and genetic screen assay development, bioinformatic analysis.

\cvsection{Core Skills/Competencies}

Molecular biology: primer design, cloning, PCR, RT-qPCR, DNA and RNA extraction for next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-Cas9 guide design, SDS-PAGE and immunoblotting, site-directed mutagenesis, Gibson assembly  
Biochemistry: protein purification from bacteria and fungi, electrophoretic-mobility shift assay, radioactivity-based ATPase assay, fluorescence polarization, magnetic-bead based biochemical assays, reconstituted eukaryotic DNA-replication initiation and elongation assays  
Yeast Genetics: genetic screen design and validation, tetrad dissections, bulk-segregant analysis, transformations and endogenous tagging using PCR- and CRISPR-based genome editing, drug-sensitivity assays, flow-cytometry analysis  
Bioinformatics: multiple-sequence alignment, PyMOL, analysis of whole-genome sequencing data (alignment, variant calling, ChIP-seq, track visualization, peak calling, motif analysis)  
Programming: R (genomic data visualization and analysis with Gviz, quantitative figure generation), Bash (NGS pipeline development on Linux HPC: Bowtie2, SAMtools, deeptools), Python; additional tools include ImageJ  
Cell Biology: mammalian cell line culture, electroporation of CRISPR-Cas9-gRNA complexes, Beacon Optofluidic System  

\cvsection{Experience}

\uline{MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (MIT)} Cambridge, MA
\vspace{-0.1em}

Post-doctoral researcher (2023--present)

Advisor: Stephen P. Bell

\textit{In vivo} characterization of ATPase-deficient origin recognition complex allele and its suppressors using chromatin immunoprecipitation.

- Designed and executed ChIP-seq experiments to characterize genomic occupancy of ATPase-deficient ORC and suppressor mutants
- Developed computational pipeline for ChIP-seq analysis: read alignment with contamination filtering, signal normalization, peak calling, and motif analysis using Bash on Linux HPC
- Genome-wide binding data visualization and statistical analysis using R
- Cloned and tested a panel of loss-of-function alleles in general transcription factors to evaluate suppression of the \textit{orc4-R267A} lethal phenotype

Graduate student researcher (2016--2023)

Advisor: Stephen P. Bell

Use of genetic and biochemical assays to understand the function of ATP hydrolysis by the origin-recognition complex during \textit{Saccharomyces cerevisiae} DNA replication.

- Design and validation of a genetic screen for suppressors of a lethal allele of the \textit{ORC4} gene. Performed the analysis of the whole-genome sequencing data.
- Genetic characterization of the suppressor alleles through CRISPR-Cas9 genetic engineering, allele-specificity and drug-sensitivity experiments.
- Biochemical characterization of an ATPase-deficient mutant ORC protein with and without the suppressor mutations.
\cvsection{Additional Research Experience}

\uline{MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (MIT)}, Cambridge, MA

Undergraduate summer research assistant (2015)

- Characterizing Bfl1-selective PUMA-like peptides derived from yeast surface display using fluorescence polarization

\uline{UNIVERSITY OF NORTH CAROLINA AT CHAPEL HILL (UNC)}, Chapel Hill, NC

Undergraduate summer research assistant (2014)

- Characterizing minimal aa-tRNA synthetase ATPase domains derived from opposite strands of a gene

\uline{UNIVERSITY OF PUERTO RICO-RIO PIEDRAS (UPR-RP)}, San Juan, PR

Undergraduate research assistant (2013--2015)

- Lipidic sponge phase crystallization of the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor derived from \textit{Torpedo californica}

Undergraduate research assistant (2012--2013)

- Aerogel synthesis derived from ash of native algae around the island of Puerto Rico

\cvsection{Education}

Ph.D. in Biology, \uline{MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (MIT)}, Cambridge, MA, 2023

Advisor: Stephen P. Bell

Thesis: Leveraging Genetic and Biochemical Approaches to Understand the Role of ORC ATP Hydrolysis in \textit{Saccharomyces cerevisiae}

B.Sc. in Cellular and Molecular Biology, \uline{UNIVERSITY OF PUERTO RICO-RIO PIEDRAS (UPR-RP)}, San Juan, PR, 2016

\cvsection{Publications}

\textbf{Martínez-Rodríguez, L.} & Bell, S. P. A lethal ORC ATPase mutation is suppressed by alterations in ORC subunits and RNA Pol II transcription components. \textit{In preparation.}

\textbf{Martínez-Rodríguez, L.}, Erdogan, O., Jimenez-Rodriguez, M., Gonzalez-Rivera, K., Williams, T., Li, L., & Carter, C. W. (2015). Functional Class I and II Amino Acid-activating Enzymes Can Be Coded by Opposite Strands of the Same Gene. \textit{Journal of Biological Chemistry}, 290(32), 19710--19725. http://doi.org/10.1074/jbc.M115.642876

\cvsection{Presentations}

New England Biolabs, Unorthodox Genomes Meeting. Poster Presentation, March 2026.

Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, Eukaryotic DNA Replication & Genome Maintenance. September 2025.

MIT Cell Biology Supergroup. Oral presentation. Cambridge, MA, March 2018.

Department of Biology Building 68 Annual Summer Retreat. Oral presentation. Plymouth, MA, June 2021.

Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, Single Biomolecules. Poster presentation. September 2018.

\cvsection{Teaching Experience}
\uline{MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (MIT)}, Cambridge, MA \hfill 2017, 2020

Teaching assistant, MIT Department of Biology

- Led biweekly recitation sessions, graded assignments and exams, and contributed to assignment and exam writing for introductory biology (7.012) and biochemistry (7.05) courses.

\cvsection{Service}

\uline{MASSACHUSETTS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (MIT)}, Cambridge, MA \hfill 2016--2023

- MIT Biology Summer Research Program: Application review committee member (2018--2023); research mentor for one undergraduate student (2019); weekly advising sessions for underrepresented undergraduate researchers (2017--2020)
- BioRefs Volunteer Group (2017--2020): Conflict-management training, organized monthly community events, served as confidential resource for graduate students
- Building 68 Data-and-Drinks Seminar Series (2017--2020): Organized biweekly seminars for graduate student and postdoctoral presentations
